pattern: A battery far bigger than the average of everything else can't be shared usefully -- set it aside to power one computer alone and keep dividing what's left, either by binary-searching the answer or by peeling off outliers one at a time.
hints: Picture the answer as a duration you're searching for: pick a candidate number of minutes and ask whether the batteries could possibly sustain all n computers for that long.
No single battery can ever contribute more than the candidate duration's worth of minutes to the total, no matter how big it is -- cap each battery's contribution at that duration before summing.

That feasibility check only gets harder to satisfy as the candidate duration grows, so binary search over it; equivalently, sort the batteries and repeatedly set aside whichever one is too large to be usefully shared given how many computers are still left.

edge_inputs.description: a single computer with a single battery
more identical batteries than computers, splitting evenly
exactly as many batteries as computers, no spares to swap in
smallest legal battery capacity, one per computer
one battery is far larger than the rest and can't be fully shared
a single dominant battery sitting among many equal tiny ones
one computer with the smallest legal battery value

stress.description: random computer counts and battery capacities across the full constraint range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 100000)))
    m = rng.randint(n, max(n, min(size, 100000)))
    batteries = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(m)]
    return [n, batteries]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def maxRunTime(self, n, batteries):
        # Independent direct approach (no binary search): sort batteries
        # descending and repeatedly set aside whichever remaining battery
        # is too large to be usefully shared given how many computers are
        # still unaccounted for; what's left splits evenly.
        ordered = sorted(batteries, reverse=True)
        total = sum(ordered)
        remaining = n
        i = 0
        while i < len(ordered) and remaining > 0 and ordered[i] * remaining > total:
            total -= ordered[i]
            remaining -= 1
            i += 1
        return total // remaining
